preamble: A direct shift from the bilabial [b] to the velar [g] requires a jump across the vocal tract. It usually occurs when a palatal glide [j] or high vowel [i] follows the [b], raising the back of the tongue toward the velum until the labial closure is lost entirely.

examples.note: The palatalized bilabial shifted its primary articulation to the velum/palate.
Velarization of a simple labial stop, leading to a doubly articulated labial-velar.
